topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance stared at the object resting on her cluttered desk. It was a rather gaudy, oversized ceramic mug, emblazoned with a cartoon depiction of a very smug-looking badger. Eleanor pinched the bridge of her nose, the faint ache throbbing between her brows. She had just spent three hours grading essays on 19th-century literature, and now this arrived. A small, handwritten note was tucked beneath it, bearing the familiar, looping script of Julian Thorne, a student she’d tried very hard to forget. "Just a little something to brighten your day, Professor," the note read. Eleanor sighed, a long, slow release of air that seemed to deflate her shoulders. She picked up the mug, the glaze cool against her fingertips, and wondered, with a weary resignation that settled deep in her chest, what entirely misplaced sentimentality this was.

[story 2]

"Honestly," Marcus muttered under his breath, meticulously stacking the ungraded papers into a precarious tower. He rubbed his temples, the fluorescent lights of the faculty lounge suddenly feeling too bright, too harsh. Another unsolicited item had appeared on his worn leather armchair. It was a vintage vinyl record, a rare pressing of a forgotten jazz artist. Attached was a card, simple and earnest. Marcus felt a distinct tightening in his jaw. He glanced at the inscription: "Thinking of you, Marcus. Hope you're doing well." He didn't even need to read further. He just wanted the silence to return, the quiet space where grading and lesson planning could exist without interruption. He picked up the record, its weight oddly heavy, and placed it on the edge of the desk, a silent acknowledgment of the intrusion.

[story 3]

Dr. Anya Sharma watched the package sit on her entryway table, a brightly colored box that seemed entirely out of place amidst the stacks of overdue library books. She ran a hand through her already disheveled hair, the gesture tight and jerky. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, was a collection of artisanal tea blends. A card informed her it was from Liam O’Connell, a student from her advanced philosophy seminar three years prior. Anya let out a small, involuntary sound—a puff of air escaping her lips—and turned back to the stack of marked exams. The sheer audacity of the gesture, the sudden demand for attention when she was already drowning in administrative tasks, made her want to simply shove the box into a drawer and forget the whole bizarre exchange had ever happened.
